content.heading: Thread Lifecycle & Synchronization
paragraphs: A Java thread goes through 6 states: NEW (created, not started), RUNNABLE (executing or ready), BLOCKED (waiting for monitor lock), WAITING (waiting indefinitely — wait(), join()), TIMED_WAITING (waiting with timeout — sleep(), wait(ms)), and TERMINATED (finished). Understanding these states is crucial for debugging deadlocks and thread dumps.

The synchronized keyword provides mutual exclusion. When a thread enters a synchronized block, it acquires the monitor (intrinsic lock) of the specified object. Any other thread trying to acquire the same lock will be BLOCKED. synchronized ensures both mutual exclusion and memory visibility — changes made inside a synchronized block are visible to the next thread acquiring the same lock.

content.title: Synchronization Patterns

content.heading: volatile vs synchronized
paragraphs: volatile guarantees visibility — writes to a volatile variable are immediately visible to all threads. It also prevents instruction reordering around the volatile access. However, volatile does NOT provide atomicity — count++ on a volatile int is still not thread-safe (it's read-modify-write, not a single operation).

synchronized provides both visibility AND atomicity — it creates a happens-before relationship between the lock release and the next lock acquisition. Use volatile for simple flags (boolean running), use synchronized or AtomicXxx for compound operations.

content.heading: ReentrantLock: More Control Than synchronized
paragraphs: ReentrantLock (java.util.concurrent.locks) is an explicit lock that provides features synchronized can't: tryLock() (non-blocking attempt), tryLock(timeout) (timed attempt), lockInterruptibly() (interrupt while waiting), and fairness (FIFO order). It also supports multiple Condition objects for selective notification.

The critical rule: always use lock() in a try-finally block with unlock() in finally. Forgetting to unlock causes permanent deadlocks. ReentrantLock is also required for virtual threads — synchronized pins the carrier thread, but ReentrantLock allows proper unmounting.

content.title: ReentrantLock vs synchronized

content.heading: Atomic Classes: Lock-Free Thread Safety
paragraphs: java.util.concurrent.atomic provides lock-free, thread-safe classes that use CAS (Compare-And-Swap) operations at the CPU level. AtomicInteger, AtomicLong, AtomicBoolean, AtomicReference are the most common. CAS atomically reads, compares with expected value, and writes the new value — all in a single CPU instruction.

For counters, use AtomicInteger.incrementAndGet(). For accumulators, use LongAdder (Java 8+) — it's faster under high contention because it distributes updates across multiple cells. For complex state machines, use AtomicReference with compareAndSet().

content.title: Atomic Classes and CAS

content.heading: wait()/notify() — Thread Coordination
paragraphs: wait(), notify(), and notifyAll() are methods on Object (not Thread) used for inter-thread communication. They MUST be called inside a synchronized block on the same object. wait() releases the lock and puts the thread into WAITING state. notify() wakes up one waiting thread. notifyAll() wakes up all waiting threads.

Critical rule: always call wait() inside a while loop (not if), because spurious wakeups can occur — the thread may be woken without notify() being called. The while loop re-checks the condition. notifyAll() is generally preferred over notify() because notify() wakes only one arbitrary thread — if the wrong thread wakes up, the signal is lost.

content.title: Producer-Consumer with wait/notify

content.heading: ReadWriteLock & StampedLock
paragraphs: ReadWriteLock allows multiple concurrent readers OR a single writer — but not both. This is ideal when reads vastly outnumber writes (e.g., configuration caches, lookup tables). ReentrantReadWriteLock is the standard implementation. Multiple threads can hold the read lock simultaneously, but the write lock is exclusive.

StampedLock (Java 8) adds optimistic reading — a try that doesn't acquire a lock at all. You read data, then validate that no write occurred during the read. If validation fails, you fall back to a pessimistic read lock. This is faster than ReadWriteLock for read-heavy workloads because optimistic reads have zero locking overhead. StampedLock is NOT reentrant — don't use from recursive code.

content.title: ReadWriteLock & StampedLock

content.heading: ThreadLocal — Per-Thread State
paragraphs: ThreadLocal<T> provides each thread with its own independent copy of a variable. Common uses: SimpleDateFormat (not thread-safe — give each thread its own), database connections per request, user context in web frameworks, transaction context.

Critical pitfall: ThreadLocal + thread pools = memory leaks. When a thread is returned to the pool, its ThreadLocal values persist. If the value references large objects or classloader-specific objects (in app servers), they're never GC'd. ALWAYS call ThreadLocal.remove() in a finally block after use, especially in server environments.

content.title: ThreadLocal Usage & Pitfalls

points: 6 thread states: NEW, RUNNABLE, BLOCKED, WAITING, TIMED_WAITING, TERMINATED
synchronized provides mutual exclusion + memory visibility
volatile provides memory visibility only — NOT atomicity
count++ is NOT atomic even with volatile — use AtomicInteger
Always synchronize on a private final object — never on 'this', String literals, or Integer
wait()/notify() MUST be in synchronized block; always use while loop for wait (spurious wakeups)
notifyAll() > notify() — notify() may wake wrong thread, losing the signal
ReadWriteLock: multiple readers OR single writer — for read-heavy workloads
StampedLock: optimistic reads (zero lock overhead) + pessimistic fallback — NOT reentrant
ThreadLocal: per-thread variable — ALWAYS remove() in finally with thread pools
ReentrantLock offers tryLock(), fairness, and interruptibility — synchronized doesn't
Always unlock ReentrantLock in a finally block — forgetting causes permanent deadlocks
AtomicInteger uses CAS — lock-free, faster than synchronized for simple counters
LongAdder is faster than AtomicLong under high contention (distributed cells)
Use ReentrantLock (not synchronized) with virtual threads to avoid carrier pinning

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between volatile and synchronized?
interviewQuestions.answer: volatile ensures visibility — any write to a volatile variable is immediately visible to all threads. But it doesn't provide atomicity — operations like count++ (read-modify-write) are not atomic with volatile. synchronized provides both visibility AND atomicity — it creates a critical section where only one thread executes at a time, and changes are flushed to main memory on lock release. Use volatile for simple flags and state variables; use synchronized for compound operations.

interviewQuestions.question: What are the conditions for a deadlock and how do you prevent it?
interviewQuestions.answer: Four conditions must hold simultaneously (Coffman conditions): (1) Mutual Exclusion — threads hold exclusive locks, (2) Hold and Wait — a thread holds a lock while waiting for another, (3) No Preemption — locks can't be forcibly taken, (4) Circular Wait — A waits for B, B waits for A. Prevention strategies: always acquire locks in a consistent global order (breaks circular wait), use tryLock() with timeout (breaks hold-and-wait), avoid nested locks when possible, use jstack or thread dumps for detection.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between ReentrantLock and synchronized?
interviewQuestions.answer: ReentrantLock provides features beyond synchronized: (1) tryLock() — non-blocking lock attempt, (2) tryLock(timeout) — timed attempt for deadlock prevention, (3) lockInterruptibly() — respond to interrupts while waiting, (4) Fairness — constructor flag for FIFO ordering, (5) Multiple Conditions — selective notify via newCondition(). (6) Compatible with virtual threads (synchronized pins carrier). Downsides: manual lock/unlock management (must use try-finally), more verbose. Use synchronized for simple cases, ReentrantLock when you need the advanced features.

interviewQuestions.question: How does CAS (Compare-And-Swap) work in Atomic classes?
interviewQuestions.answer: CAS is a CPU-level atomic instruction: it reads the current value, compares it with an expected value, and if they match, writes a new value — all atomically. In Java, AtomicInteger.incrementAndGet() works like: loop { int current = get(); int next = current + 1; if (compareAndSet(current, next)) return next; }. If another thread changed the value between read and CAS, the CAS fails and retries. This is lock-free (no blocking) but can spin under extreme contention — that's why LongAdder uses distributed cells for better scalability.

interviewQuestions.question: When should you use LongAdder instead of AtomicLong?
interviewQuestions.answer: Use LongAdder when multiple threads frequently increment/decrement a counter and you only need the final sum occasionally. AtomicLong's single CAS hotspot becomes a bottleneck under high contention — threads keep retrying as the value changes. LongAdder distributes updates across multiple cells (Thread-striped), reducing CAS contention. The trade-off: LongAdder uses more memory and sum() is only eventually consistent (reflects all updates only when no concurrent modification is happening). Use AtomicLong when you need immediate consistent reads.

trickyPoints.title: volatile count++ Is NOT Atomic
trickyPoints.mistake: Using volatile int count and doing count++ thinking it's thread-safe.
trickyPoints.correct: count++ is three operations: read, increment, write. volatile ensures each individual read/write is visible, but the compound operation is not atomic. Two threads can read the same value, increment independently, and write back — losing one increment. Use AtomicInteger.incrementAndGet() instead.

trickyPoints.title: Synchronizing on Wrong Object
trickyPoints.mistake: Synchronizing on 'this' (makes locking public), on String literals (shared across JVM), or on Integer/Boolean (cached values shared globally).
trickyPoints.correct: Always synchronize on a private final Object. Synchronizing on 'this' lets external code also lock your monitor, causing unexpected contention or deadlocks. String literals and cached wrapper objects are global singletons.

trickyPoints.title: Forgetting to Unlock ReentrantLock
trickyPoints.mistake: Not using try-finally with ReentrantLock, causing permanent lock if an exception occurs inside the critical section.
trickyPoints.correct: ALWAYS use lock() before try and unlock() in finally. If the critical section throws, the lock is never released without finally — causing all other threads to wait forever.

content.heading: Thread Pools: Managing Thread Lifecycle
paragraphs: Creating threads manually (new Thread()) is expensive — each thread consumes ~1MB of stack memory and OS resources for creation/context-switching. The Executor framework (java.util.concurrent) provides thread pool management, separating task submission from execution mechanics.

Executors factory methods provide common pool configurations. Under the hood, they all use ThreadPoolExecutor with different parameters: corePoolSize (minimum threads), maximumPoolSize (cap), keepAliveTime (idle thread timeout), and the work queue implementation determines overflow behavior.

content.title: Thread Pool Types

content.heading: Callable vs Runnable
paragraphs: Runnable.run() returns void and cannot throw checked exceptions. Callable<V>.call() returns a value and can throw exceptions. When you submit a Callable to an ExecutorService, you get back a Future<V> that represents the pending result. Future.get() blocks until the result is available.

Always prefer submit() over execute() for error handling. execute(Runnable) silently swallows exceptions in the thread. submit() captures exceptions and surfaces them when you call future.get() — wrapped in an ExecutionException.

content.heading: ScheduledExecutorService — Timed Tasks
paragraphs: ScheduledExecutorService runs tasks after a delay or periodically. schedule() runs once after a delay. scheduleAtFixedRate() runs at fixed intervals from the START of each execution (if execution takes longer than the period, the next execution starts immediately). scheduleWithFixedDelay() runs with a fixed delay between the END of one execution and the START of the next.

Always prefer ScheduledExecutorService over java.util.Timer — Timer uses a single thread (one slow task delays all others), throws unchecked exceptions that kill the timer thread, and doesn't handle daylight saving changes well.

content.heading: ForkJoinPool — Divide and Conquer
paragraphs: ForkJoinPool (Java 7) is designed for recursive divide-and-conquer algorithms. It uses work-stealing: each thread has its own deque of tasks. When a thread finishes its tasks, it steals tasks from other threads' deques — maximizing CPU utilization. Parallel streams use the common ForkJoinPool internally.

Extend RecursiveTask<V> (returns value) or RecursiveAction (void). Override compute(): if the task is small enough, solve directly; otherwise, split into subtasks, fork them, then join results. The threshold for splitting is critical — too small means overhead dominates, too large means poor parallelism.

content.title: ScheduledExecutor, ForkJoinPool & Rejection Policies

content.heading: BlockingQueue — Thread-Safe Producer-Consumer
paragraphs: BlockingQueue is a thread-safe queue with blocking operations. put() blocks when full (waits for space), take() blocks when empty (waits for data). This eliminates manual wait/notify coordination. It's the backbone of the Executor framework — ThreadPoolExecutor uses it as its work queue.

Implementations: ArrayBlockingQueue (bounded, fair option), LinkedBlockingQueue (optionally bounded, higher throughput), PriorityBlockingQueue (unbounded, priority-ordered), SynchronousQueue (zero capacity — direct handoff, used by CachedThreadPool), DelayQueue (elements available only after a delay — for scheduled tasks).

content.title: BlockingQueue Producer-Consumer

points: Never use new Thread() in production — use Executor framework
newFixedThreadPool: predictable resource usage, unbounded queue (risk: OOM from queued tasks)
newCachedThreadPool: auto-scaling, SynchronousQueue (risk: unbounded thread creation)
Always prefer ThreadPoolExecutor directly with bounded queue for production
Callable returns a value + throws exceptions; Runnable does neither
scheduleAtFixedRate: fixed interval from START; scheduleWithFixedDelay: fixed delay from END
ForkJoinPool uses work-stealing — idle threads steal tasks from busy threads' deques
CallerRunsPolicy is the safest rejection policy — slows down the producer (backpressure)
BlockingQueue.put() blocks when full, take() blocks when empty — no manual synchronization
Always shutdown() the executor when done — executor.shutdown() then awaitTermination()

interviewQuestions.question: Why should you avoid Executors.newFixedThreadPool() in production?
interviewQuestions.answer: newFixedThreadPool uses an unbounded LinkedBlockingQueue. If tasks are submitted faster than they're processed, the queue grows without limit, eventually causing OutOfMemoryError. In production, create a ThreadPoolExecutor directly with a bounded queue (ArrayBlockingQueue) and a rejection policy (CallerRunsPolicy is safest). This is also a recommendation in Alibaba Java coding guidelines and is commonly asked in interviews.

interviewQuestions.question: Explain the four rejection policies in ThreadPoolExecutor.
interviewQuestions.answer: When the thread pool and queue are both full: (1) AbortPolicy (default) — throws RejectedExecutionException. Good for detecting overload but crashes if not caught. (2) CallerRunsPolicy — executes the task in the calling thread. Provides natural backpressure but slows the submitter. (3) DiscardPolicy — silently drops the task. Data loss risk. (4) DiscardOldestPolicy — drops the oldest queued task and retries. CallerRunsPolicy is safest because it slows down producers and avoids data loss.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the ForkJoinPool and how does work-stealing work?
interviewQuestions.answer: ForkJoinPool is optimized for recursive divide-and-conquer tasks. Each worker thread has its own deque (double-ended queue). When a thread finishes its tasks, it steals tasks from the TAIL of other threads' deques (while the owner works from the HEAD). This maximizes parallelism with minimal contention. It's used by parallel streams internally. Extend RecursiveTask<V> (returns value) or RecursiveAction (void), and implement compute() with a fork/join pattern.

trickyPoints.title: Unbounded Queues Cause OOM
trickyPoints.mistake: Using Executors factory methods without understanding their queue types — newFixedThreadPool has an unbounded queue.
trickyPoints.correct: Always create ThreadPoolExecutor directly with a bounded queue and appropriate rejection policy. This gives you explicit control over backpressure.

content.heading: CountDownLatch, CyclicBarrier, Semaphore, Phaser
paragraphs: These are coordination utilities in java.util.concurrent for thread synchronization patterns beyond simple locks. CountDownLatch is a one-time gate — threads wait until a counter reaches zero. CyclicBarrier is a reusable meet-up point — N threads wait until all N arrive, then all proceed together.

Semaphore controls access to a shared resource by maintaining a set of permits — acquire() takes a permit (blocks if none available), release() returns one. Phaser is the most flexible — it's a reusable barrier where the number of participating threads can change dynamically.

content.title: Synchronizer Patterns
Synchronizers Comparison
rows: Wait for N tasks to complete
N threads sync at a point repeatedly

points: CountDownLatch: single-use, count goes to 0 and stays — cannot be reset
CyclicBarrier: auto-resets after all threads arrive — reusable across cycles
Semaphore: controls concurrent access count — not ownership-based like locks
Phaser: dynamic participants, multi-phase — most flexible but most complex

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between CountDownLatch and CyclicBarrier?
interviewQuestions.answer: CountDownLatch: one thread (or more) waits for N events/threads to complete. It's one-shot — once count hits 0, it can't be reused. The waiting thread and counting threads can be different. CyclicBarrier: N threads wait for each other — all must reach the barrier before any can proceed. It's reusable — automatically resets after each cycle. Can execute a barrier action when all threads arrive. Use latch when one thread waits for others; use barrier when threads mutually synchronize.

content.heading: Asynchronous Programming Done Right
paragraphs: CompletableFuture (Java 8) is Java's answer to JavaScript Promises. It represents a future result that can be chained with callbacks, composed with other futures, and doesn't block the calling thread. It implements both Future<T> (for basic get/cancel) and CompletionStage<T> (for the rich chaining API).

The key advantage over plain Future is non-blocking composition. With Future, you must call get() (blocking) to retrieve the result. With CompletableFuture, you chain transformations: thenApply(), thenAccept(), thenCompose(), and they execute asynchronously when the result becomes available.

content.title: CompletableFuture Chaining

points: supplyAsync() for tasks that return values, runAsync() for void tasks
thenApply() transforms the result (like map), thenCompose() chains futures (like flatMap)
thenAccept() consumes the result without returning, thenRun() ignores the result entirely
Async variants (thenApplyAsync) run on a different thread — non-async runs on the completing thread
exceptionally() handles errors, handle() processes both result and exception
allOf() waits for all futures, anyOf() takes the first completed
Default executor is ForkJoinPool.commonPool() — pass custom executor for I/O tasks

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between thenApply() and thenCompose()?
interviewQuestions.answer: thenApply(Function<T,U>) transforms the result and wraps it in a CompletableFuture<U> — like Stream.map(). Use when the transformation is synchronous. thenCompose(Function<T, CompletableFuture<U>>) chains to another async operation — like Stream.flatMap(). Use when the transformation itself returns a CompletableFuture. Without thenCompose, you'd get CompletableFuture<CompletableFuture<U>> — thenCompose flattens it.

trickyPoints.title: Default Executor for Async Operations
trickyPoints.mistake: Using supplyAsync() for I/O operations without a custom executor — blocks threads in the common ForkJoinPool.
trickyPoints.correct: ForkJoinPool.commonPool() is shared across the JVM and sized to CPU cores. Blocking I/O operations (HTTP calls, DB queries) should use a custom cached executor to avoid starving CPU-bound tasks.

content.heading: Scaling to Millions of Threads
paragraphs: Virtual Threads (Java 21) are lightweight threads managed by the JVM, not the OS. A platform thread maps 1:1 to an OS thread and costs ~1MB of stack memory. Virtual threads are ~1KB and are multiplexed onto a small pool of platform threads (carrier threads) by the JVM scheduler.

This means you can create millions of virtual threads without running out of memory or OS threads. They're designed for I/O-bound workloads — when a virtual thread blocks on I/O, the JVM automatically unmounts it from the carrier thread, allowing another virtual thread to run. This is transparent to the developer — no async/await syntax needed.

content.title: Virtual Threads in Action

points: Virtual threads are JVM-managed, ~1KB each — create millions without issue
Designed for I/O-bound code — blocking is handled by the JVM scheduler transparently
Don't pool virtual threads — create a new one per task (they're cheap)
Avoid synchronized blocks in virtual threads — they pin the carrier thread. Use ReentrantLock instead
Thread.currentThread().isVirtual() checks if running on a virtual thread
Executors.newVirtualThreadPerTaskExecutor() is the standard way to use them
Not faster for CPU-bound work — the benefit is scalability for I/O-bound

interviewQuestions.question: What are Virtual Threads and when should you use them?
interviewQuestions.answer: Virtual threads are lightweight (JVM-managed) threads introduced in Java 21. They're ~1KB (vs ~1MB for platform threads) and are multiplexed onto platform threads by the JVM. Use them for I/O-bound workloads (HTTP servers, database access, file I/O) where you have many concurrent tasks that spend most of their time waiting. Don't use them for CPU-bound computation — they offer no speedup there since the work is limited by CPU cores, not thread count.

trickyPoints.title: synchronized Pins Virtual Threads
trickyPoints.mistake: Using synchronized blocks in virtual-thread code — this pins the virtual thread to its carrier, blocking a platform thread.
trickyPoints.correct: Replace synchronized with ReentrantLock in code that runs on virtual threads. ReentrantLock allows proper unmounting during contention.

content.heading: Managing Subtask Lifecycles
paragraphs: Structured Concurrency (preview in Java 21) treats multiple concurrent tasks as a single unit of work. Using StructuredTaskScope, subtasks are forked within a scope and the scope doesn't complete until all subtasks finish (or are cancelled). If one subtask fails, others can be automatically cancelled.
This solves the problem of subtask leakage — where forked tasks continue running even after the parent has failed or timed out. The scope enforces the rule: subtask lifetime ≤ parent scope lifetime.

content.title: Structured Concurrency Pattern

points: StructuredTaskScope enforces subtask lifetime ≤ scope lifetime
ShutdownOnFailure: if any subtask fails, all others are cancelled
ShutdownOnSuccess: first successful result wins, rest are cancelled
Works naturally with virtual threads — fork() creates virtual threads by default
Prevents thread leakage and resource orphaning
Preview feature in Java 21 — enable with --enable-preview

interviewQuestions.question: What problem does Structured Concurrency solve?
interviewQuestions.answer: Structured Concurrency solves subtask leakage. In traditional concurrent code, if you fork two tasks and one fails, the other continues running wastefully — possibly holding resources, doing unnecessary work, or even succeeding and its result being silently discarded. With StructuredTaskScope, the scope automatically cancels remaining subtasks when one fails (ShutdownOnFailure) or when the first succeeds (ShutdownOnSuccess). It enforces the rule that subtask lifetimes are bounded by their parent scope.

content.heading: A Better ThreadLocal
paragraphs: Scoped Values (preview in Java 21) are a lightweight alternative to ThreadLocal. While ThreadLocal allows any code to set/get a mutable per-thread value (which often leads to memory leaks and unclear lifecycles), ScopedValue provides immutable, bounded-scope, inheritable values.

A ScopedValue is set for a specific scope of execution using ScopedValue.runWhere() — it's automatically unset when the scope exits. Child virtual threads inherit the value. Unlike ThreadLocal.remove() which developers forget to call, ScopedValue cleanup is automatic.

content.title: ScopedValue vs ThreadLocal

points: ScopedValue is immutable within its scope — no accidental mutation
Automatic cleanup when scope exits — no memory leaks
Inherited by child virtual threads (ThreadLocal requires InheritableThreadLocal)
Designed for virtual threads — ThreadLocal copies are expensive with millions of threads
Preview feature in Java 21

interviewQuestions.question: Why is ScopedValue better than ThreadLocal?
interviewQuestions.answer: ScopedValue improvements: (1) Immutability — can't be accidentally modified mid-scope. (2) Automatic cleanup — no memory leaks from forgotten remove() calls. (3) Bounded lifetime — value is only accessible within runWhere() scope. (4) Efficient with virtual threads — ThreadLocal uses per-thread storage which is expensive when you have millions of virtual threads. (5) Naturally inherited by child virtual threads without InheritableThreadLocal. ThreadLocal's problems: mutable state, manual cleanup, memory leaks, expensive cloning for thread pools.

content.heading: Happens-Before & Memory Visibility
paragraphs: The Java Memory Model (JMM) defines how threads interact through memory. Each thread has a local working memory (CPU registers, caches) and shared main memory. Without explicit synchronization, there's no guarantee that a write by thread A is visible to thread B — the JVM and CPU are free to cache, buffer, and reorder operations.

The happens-before relationship defines the visibility rules: if action A happens-before action B, then A's results are guaranteed visible to B. Key happens-before rules: program order within a thread, monitor lock/unlock, volatile write/read, thread start/join, and final field initialization in constructors.

content.heading: Instruction Reordering
paragraphs: The JVM and CPU can reorder instructions for performance as long as the reordering is undetectable within a single thread (as-if-serial semantics). But in multi-threaded code, reordering can cause unexpected behavior.

The classic example: the double-checked locking pattern for singletons. Without volatile on the instance field, the JVM can reorder the constructor call — another thread might see a partially constructed object. volatile prevents this reordering by establishing a happens-before relationship.

content.title: Double-Checked Locking (Correct)

points: JMM defines happens-before rules for thread memory visibility
Without synchronization, threads may see stale values from their local cache
volatile establishes happens-before: write < read across threads
synchronized: lock release < subsequent lock acquire
final fields are safely published after constructor completes
Instruction reordering is allowed within a thread but can break multi-threaded logic
Double-checked locking requires volatile — without it, partially constructed objects can be seen

interviewQuestions.question: Explain the happens-before relationship in JMM.
interviewQuestions.answer: Happens-before defines when one thread's changes are guaranteed visible to another. Key rules: (1) Program Order — within a thread, earlier actions happen-before later ones. (2) Monitor Lock — unlock happens-before subsequent lock on the same monitor. (3) Volatile — write to volatile variable happens-before subsequent read. (4) Thread Start — Thread.start() happens-before any action in the new thread. (5) Thread Join — actions in a thread happen-before join() returns. (6) Final Fields — writing a final field in a constructor happens-before any read of that field after construction.

trickyPoints.title: Double-Checked Locking Without Volatile
trickyPoints.mistake: Implementing singleton double-checked locking without volatile — leads to partially constructed object visibility.
trickyPoints.correct: Always mark the singleton instance as volatile. Without it, the JVM may reorder the allocation, reference assignment, and constructor execution — allowing another thread to see instance != null but an uninitialized object.
